title: Spotted dick
subtitle: British steamed suet pudding with currants
excerpt: A classic British steamed suet pudding with currants throughout, made from self-raising flour, shredded suet, and milk. Steamed for two hours until light and spongy, served hot with custard. The 'spots' are the currants; the name is Victorian.
sourceNotes: Spotted dick is one of the most well-documented British steamed puddings. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records several suet pudding recipes with dried fruit under similar names; the combination of suet pastry and currants is documented from at least the mid-nineteenth century. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), documents boiled suet puddings with dried fruit. The name 'spotted dick' appears in Victorian recipe collections; the 'spotted' refers to the currants, and 'dick' is likely a corruption of 'dough' or a regional word. The self-raising flour method became standard from the 1850s onwards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Steam fully, cool, wrap tightly, and freeze in the basin. Steam for 1 hour from frozen (thawed) or 1.5 hours from fully frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for three days if stored cool; reheat by steaming for 30 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Steamed over simmering water for 2 hours. The water level must stay at least a third of the way up the basin at all times; check every 30 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A correctly steamed suet pudding has a light, spongy crumb — quite different from the dense, stodgy texture associated with badly made versions. The self-raising flour provides lift; the suet melts into the batter as it steams, creating small pockets that give the pudding its characteristic texture. The pudding should spring back when pressed on the surface and pull away from the sides of the basin. A pudding that is wet and heavy in the centre has not steamed long enough.

content.text: A well-made spotted dick has a

that is light and spongy, not dense. The suet is the key ingredient: shredded beef or vegetable suet coats the flour particles, preventing gluten from developing and producing the characteristic tender, short crumb. Two hours of gentle steaming is what achieves this; a vigorous boil toughens it. Serve with proper hot custard or, at a stretch, vanilla ice cream.

Butter a 1 litre pudding basin. Mix the flour, suet, sugar, currants, and salt in a large bowl. Beat the egg with the milk and stir into the dry ingredients until you have a soft, dropping batter. It should fall off the spoon in thick clumps rather than pouring. Spoon into the prepared basin. Cut a circle of baking paper slightly larger than the top of the basin; press it directly onto the surface of the batter. Cover the basin with a double layer of baking paper and a layer of foil, pleating a fold in the centre to allow expansion. Secure tightly with kitchen string around the rim.

Place the basin in a large saucepan. Pour in boiling water to come halfway up the sides. Cover, bring to a gentle simmer, and cook for 2 hours, checking the water level every 30 minutes. The pudding is done when the surface is set, a skewer through the coverings comes out clean, and the pudding has pulled away from the sides. Turn out onto a warmed plate and serve at once.
